gmc_consult1_name: Public consultation on the Draft Law on Artificial Intelligence
gmc_consult1_type: Open public consultation (e.g. online comments public hearing)
gmc_consult1_justif: The public consultation platform of the Ministry of Telecommunications Information Technologies and Social Communication enabled CSO participation by providing an official channel for submitting feedback on the AI policy and other sectoral draft  policies. The consultation process is described as a mechanism for dialogue between public administration and society allowing communities and organised groups to contribute information and opinions to improve the documents under review. According to the consultation guidance the population including CSOs may comment on issues of collective interest and support government decision-making by expanding the discussion (Public Consultation Portal - “Consulta Pública” section).

gmc_title: Policy for the responsible use of AI in government (Australia)
gmc_defence_and_security_justif: The policy explicitly excludes defence and national intelligence activities from its scope. Under the “National security carveouts” section it states that the policy “does not apply to the use of AI in the defence portfolio” and does not apply to the “national intelligence community (NIC)” including agencies such as ASIO ASD ASIS and others (p.8). It further notes that Defence and NIC entities may only voluntarily adopt elements of the policy where appropriate.
gmc_particip_mech1_name: The establishment of a new Artificial Intelligence Expert Group
gmc_particip_mech1_type: Oversight advisory policy monitoring and evaluation bodies where CSOs have a seat
gmc_particip_mech1_justif: The Artificial Intelligence Expert Group provides advice to the Department of Industry Science and Resources on AI governance issues including transparency testing accountability andguardrails for high-risk AI systems. The group includes a representative from the Gradient Institute an independent non-profit research organisation focused on ethical and responsible AI. Through this membership the mechanism enables civil society participation by allowing a CSO-affiliated expert to contribute to government discussions and provide input into the development of Australia’s AI governance approaches.

gmc_consult1_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult1_justif: The strategy enabled CSO participation by involving civil society actors in its development and implementation framework. The document states that more than 160 experts from diverse disciplines including civil society organisations were involved in drafting the strategy and that it is a participatory interdisciplinary process open to the involvement of civil society and citizens (pg. 3-4). This shows CSO involvement in shaping the AI policy.
gmc_particip_mech1_name: AI Stakeholder Forum
gmc_particip_mech1_type: Oversight advisory policy monitoring and evaluation bodies where CSOs have a seat
gmc_particip_mech1_justif: The AI Stakeholder Forum enables CSO participation and oversight in Austria’s AI governance by serving as a formal multi-stakeholder body where civil society organisations sit alongside industry academia and social partners. Through regular consultations feedback rounds and structured dialogue with the federal government CSOs can review comment on and influence the implementation of national AI strategies and Austria’s approach to the EU AI Act. This gives civil society a direct channel to shape policy decisions raise risks or societal concerns and monitor how AI-related measures are carried out.

gmc_title: AI Stakeholders in Belgium: Steering Commitee for the National Convergence Plan for the Development of Artificial Intelligence (Belgium)
gmc_consult1_name: Operational Federal Data and AI Ethics Advisory Committee
gmc_consult1_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult1_justif: CSOs appear to have been involved in the broader ecosystem surrounding the development of the Plan national de convergence pour le développement de l’intelligence artificielle. The list of actors includes civil society–relevant bodies such as the Conseil national du travail which represents social partners and issues opinions to government and Parliament as well as think tanks and knowledge centres like The Data Tank and The Knowledge Centre Data & Society which engage with civil society and public interest issues. Given their inclusion among the actors contributing to the AI ecosystem and governance structure CSO-type actors were involved in the policy development process. See question 49.
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: The AI4Belgium coalition is explicitly included in the policy's governance structure for ongoing input and monitoring. The coalition is the primary multi-stakeholder body (p. 35-36 framework: National Plan).
gmc_particip_provis2_type: AI observatory or ethics committee with CSO representation
gmc_particip_provis2_justif: The Federal Data and AI Ethics Advisory Committee was established. The Plan also refers to the establishment of an AI Observatory to track implementation which is another type of monitoring mechanism that civil society can engage with (p. 35-36 framework: National Plan). Its composition explicitly includes two representatives of the National Labour Council which represents social partners (trade unions and employer organisations).
gmc_particip_mech1_name: Steering Committee
gmc_particip_mech1_type: Oversight advisory policy monitoring and evaluation bodies where CSOs have a seat
gmc_particip_mech1_justif: To guide the implementation of this national plan a joint Steering Committee was created between FPS BOSA and FPS Economy composed of 12 members 6 French-speaking and 6 Dutch-speaking (p. 37). The Steering Committee will function as a single point of contact at the federal level for AI-related issues. If deemed necessary the Steering Committee may create a subgroup with a mixed composition (members of the political committee and external members). This information is in the main framework p.35-36.

gmc_title: National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence and Big Data 2023-2027
gmc_body_name: Ministry of Digital Affairs and Digitalisation (lead coordinating body) supported by the Steering Committee Technical Committee and Program Support Units as set out under the Implementation Mechanism (Chapter 4.1 pp.42–43)
gmc_consult1_name: Process of Developing National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence and Big Data (2023–2027)
gmc_consult1_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult1_justif: The Strategy states that it was developed by the Ministry of Digital Affairs and Digitalization using a “stakeholder-integrated approach” (Introduction p. 2). Within its Theory of Change it explicitly identifies “Civil Society Leaders” among the actors bearing governance and accountability responsibilities (p. 13). More specifically the development process of Benin’s National Artificial Intelligence and Big Data Strategy (SNIAM) is described as integrating stakeholders through a holistic approach centered on technological solutions tailored to the country’s specific needs. This is still continuing over the monitoring stage of the strategy.
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: The policy does not establish a formal “advisory council” “AI observatory/ethics committee” or audit/impact body with explicit CSO seats. Instead it foresees multi-stakeholder participation through the implementation mechanism: the coordinating body involves “all stakeholders” (Implementation Mechanism p.42) and “ecosystem stakeholders” are “involved in the implementation of the actions” and “express their needs and give their opinions” (Responsibilities of Actors p.43)

gmc_title: Draft National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (2025-2030) (Cambodia)
gmc_consult1_name: Progress of Preparing and Opening Public Consultation on Draft National Artificial Intelligence Strategy
gmc_consult1_drive: As the provided link is the announcement by the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications (MPTC) we cannot upload relevant documents onto the drive.
gmc_consult1_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult1_justif: The initiative supported CSO participation by openly inviting CSOs to review and comment on the draft National AI Strategy providing public access to the documents and a structured feedback form and holding a multi-stakeholder workshop that included CSOs. The ministry also committed to reviewing and integrating all submitted feedback into the final strategy ensuring CSOs had opportunities to influence the policy.
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: The draft Strategy outlines a 'National AI Observatory' (Strategic Priority 5 Flagship Activity 4) to monitor ethical and responsible AI adoption. Including CSO representation is justified because Strategic Priority 6 commits to a “multi-stakeholder approach” involving civil society in implementation (Section III p.16-17; Measures 6.2 and 6.6). Embedding CSOs in the Observatory or ethics bodies strengthens transparency aligns with the Strategy’s collaboration principles and ensures societal perspectives are reflected in monitoring.
gmc_particip_provis2_type: AI observatory or ethics committee with CSO representation
gmc_particip_provis2_justif: The Strategy repeatedly mandates a 'multi-stakeholder approach' making this mechanism well aligned with its design. Sec.III (Strategic Priority 6 p.16-17) requires creating an environment for collaboration among government private sector academia and 'civil society' (Measures 6.2 and 6.6). The Conclusion (p.18) further states that implementation depends on cooperation with CSOs. Using multi-stakeholder mechanisms therefore reflects the Strategy’s intent and ensures inclusive representative implementation.
gmc_particip_mech1_name: The Fourth Cambodia ICT and Digital Forum: Dialogue on the Digital Development
gmc_particip_mech1_drive: As the provided link is an article by CamIDF we cannot upload relevant documents onto the drive.
gmc_particip_mech1_type: Other
gmc_particip_mech1_justif: The Fourth CamIDF enables CSO participation in AI governance by providing a multi-stakeholder forum where civil society can engage directly with government private sector and technical experts. Through open discussions on AI ethics data protection and digital rights CSOs can raise concerns influence policy directions and strengthen oversight. The forum’s focus on knowledge-sharing and cooperation also builds CSO capacity to monitor and shape future AI governance.

gmc_title: Côte d'Ivoire's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (SNIA) for 2030
gmc_body_name: National Artificial Intelligence Agency/Agence National de l'IA (ANIA)
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: SNIA 2030 explicitly provides for a multi-stakeholder advisory board that clearly includes civil society organizations (CSOs) within the National Artificial Intelligence Council (CNIA). The CNIA is presented as a permanent advisory body attached to the National Artificial Intelligence Agency. It is responsible for ensuring inclusive and participatory governance. Specific references in the official document: Page 57 (section “Governance” / “Governance bodies”) (Page 58 (subsection “Composition of the CNIA”). The SNIA specifies that 20% of members must come from CSOs. These provisions aim to ensure inclusive governance that is representative of social diversity and attentive to ethical inclusion and equity issues.
gmc_particip_provis2_type: CSO membership in audit or impact assessment bodies
gmc_particip_provis2_justif: SNIA 2030 explicitly provides for the participation/involvement of CSOs in the bodies or mechanisms responsible for auditing and evaluating the impact of the AI strategy and projects. In fact CSOs must be part of the steering committee for each independent audit (minimum quota of 15-20%). They must participate in defining the terms of reference selecting the firm and validating the final report of the Independent Audits on AI technological solutions to be deployed and/or currently in use. Audéka it is also planned that any algorithmic impact assessment (AIA) or social impact assessment may include at least one CSO representing vulnerable groups in the panel of assessors.

gmc_title: Organic Law Project for the Regulation and Promotion of Artificial Intelligence in Ecuador
gmc_consult1_name: Multi-sectoral technical committee and deliberative consultation process for the development of the Organic Law on Artificial Intelligence
gmc_consult1_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult1_justif: The draft Organic Law on Artificial Intelligence was developed through a multi-actor deliberative process that included civil society organizations indigenous peoples feminist groups consumer associations academia and the private sector. Page 6 of the Draft Law states that the regulatory vision resulted from an “intense deliberative process” involving civil society actors. Page 79 provides that a multi-sectoral technical committee including representatives of public institutions academia the private sector and civil society must participate in the preparation of the National AI Strategy. The framework also promotes ongoing multisectoral participation in governance and policy updates (Art.45).
gmc_particip_provis1_type: AI observatory or ethics committee with CSO representation
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: The draft law establishes formal mechanisms for civil society participation in AI governance and oversight. Art.49 creates the National Ethics Committee in Artificial Intelligence Research (CNEI-IA) composed of experts and representatives of civil society to review AI research with potential human rights impacts. Art.65 establishes a Citizen Observatory of AI and Justice including universities bar associations civil society organizations consumers and users of the justice system to monitor the use and impacts of AI in the justice sector. In addition Art.45 requires that updates to the National AI Strategy occur through a participatory multi-stakeholder process including civil society.

gmc_consult1_name: Participation in committee on generative artificial intelligence
gmc_consult1_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult1_justif: The 'Generative AI Committee' was formally tasked by the Prime Minister to 'present concrete proposals' to 'adapt our national strategy' (p. 1). Its composition explicitly ensured multi-stakeholder participation by including civil society representatives like Franca Salis-Madinier (CFDT Cadres union p. 2) and Martin Tisné (Open Government Partnership p. 2). The committee functioned as a diverse working group to 'enlighten Government decisions' (p. 1)  ensuring that non-state perspectives on 'societal' and 'ethical' issues (p. 1)  directly shaped the strategic recommendations delivered to the government.
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: The Phase 3 National Strategy explicitly mandates the National Digital Council (CNNum) to lead ongoing civil society participation. On page 26 the strategy tasks this independent consultative commission with 'structuring a system of democratic debates' (Café IA) and 'bringing together stakeholders... to collectively build' AI awareness initiatives. Additionally the Prime Minister's editorial (p. 3) and the 'Empowering the Nation' section (p. 25) establish a formal 'social dialogue' mechanism involving 'professional trade unions' to co-develop regulations and oversee AI's impact on work ensuring a permanent channel for non-state actor inclusion in governance.
gmc_particip_provis2_type: AI observatory or ethics committee with CSO representation
gmc_particip_provis2_justif: The Phase 3 Strategy establishes two key oversight bodies: INESIA (National Institute for AI Assessment and Security p. 25) for technical evaluation and LaborIA (p. 36) a dedicated 'action-research laboratory' to 'analyze and experiment with the impacts of AI technologies on work' LaborIA is explicitly tasked with 'fostering public debate on the major social implications of AI.' Crucially the strategy mandates that these oversight mechanisms operate within a framework of 'social dialogue' that formally involves 'workers and unions' (CSOs) in 'developing regulations and disseminating use' (p. 3 36) ensuring civil society representation in monitoring AI's societal impact.
gmc_particip_provis3_type: CSO membership in audit or impact assessment bodies
gmc_particip_provis3_justif: The Phase 3 Strategy explicitly designates LaborIA (p. 36) as the national body responsible for 'analysing and experimenting with the impacts of AI technologies on work.' While LaborIA leads the technical assessment the strategy mandates that this oversight be conducted through a formal 'social dialogue' involving Trade Unions (CSOs). The Prime Minister’s editorial (p. 3) and the governance section (p. 25) state that 'Harnessing AI means involving all of society (workers and unions) in developing its regulations' thereby ensuring that civil society representatives are active participants in the bodies assessing AI's societal and labour impacts.
gmc_particip_mech1_name: Artificial Intelligence and Digital Council
gmc_particip_mech1_type: Oversight advisory policy monitoring and evaluation bodies where CSOs have a seat
gmc_particip_mech1_justif: The Council institutionalizes the participation of Civil Society Organizations by reserving eight of its 17 seats for 'qualified personalities' from the associative sector and civil society. It is statutorily mandated to consult civil society regularly to advise the government on the societal and economic impacts of Artificial Intelligence. This mechanism provides an independent permanent platform for non-state actors to formulate recommendations and oversee policy development to ensure technological progress aligns with the general interest.
gmc_particip_mech2_justif: Le LaborIA is a research-action laboratory co-led by the Ministry of Labour and Inria. It facilitates Civil Society Organization participation through its Executive Committee (COMEX) which explicitly includes 'qualified personalities' recognized for their expertise in Artificial Intelligence and work-related issues. The mechanism supports participation by producing pedagogical tools and publications for 'organisations syndicales' (trade unions) and employee representatives. By integrating these stakeholders into research projects and public debates the initiative ensures that social partners and non-state actors can evaluate risks and influence the conditions for integrating Artificial Intelligence into professional environments to protec

gmc_title: Draft Bill of the Federal Ministry for Digital Affairs and State Modernization (Germany)
gmc_defence_and_security_justif: Section 1 (p. 4) explicitly adopts the scope of Article 2 of the EU AI Act which excludes AI systems used exclusively for military defense or national security purposes. This exclusion is based on Recital 24 and the TEU acknowledging that national security remains the exclusive competence of Member States.
gmc_consult1_name: Open Consultations and Public Comments on the AI Market Surveillance Act
gmc_consult1_type: Open public consultation (e.g. online comments public hearing)
gmc_consult1_justif: The AI Market Surveillance Act (KIMÜG) supported CSO participation through the formal association consultation (Länder- und Verbändeanhörung) initiated on September 12 2025. This process enabled CSOs to provide expert technical and legal feedback through a structured questionnaire and written submissions (https://www.lobbyregister.bundestag.de/media/a8/2b/659360/Stellungnahme-Gutachten-SG2512170092.pdf). AlgorithmWatch is a notable German CSO that took part in the consultation: https://www.lobbyregister.bundestag.de/media/f6/2d/628462/Lobbyregister-Regelungsvorhaben-Detailansicht-RV0004864-2025-10-13_12-24-40.pdf  full statement: https://algorithmwatch.org/de/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/251009b-KI-MIG-Stellungnahme-AlgorithmWatch.pdf
gmc_particip_mech1_name: EU AI Act Advisory Forum
gmc_particip_mech1_type: Oversight advisory policy monitoring and evaluation bodies where CSOs have a seat
gmc_particip_mech1_justif: Following the entry into force of the EU AI Act there was an open call for applications to the EU AI Act Advisory Forum. The Forum includes dedicated seats for civil society to advise the AI Office and the AI Board. The selected participants will be announced in early 2026. German CSOs such as AlgorithmWatch have been vocal about the being involved in shaping the Advisory Forum (https://politiscope.eu/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/Politiscope_Open_Joint_Letter_against_the_Delaying_and_Reopening_of_the_AI_Act.pdf  p.3). Because the KI-MIG is a Regulation-implementation law Germany will have to follow any technical consensus from the Forum once established.

gmc_consult1_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult1_justif: The national Ministry of Economy Trade and Industry (METI) and the Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications (MIC) opened a public opinion call on the E-GOV platform for the “Draft AI Business Guidelines” in which 47 corporations and organisations submitted comments to inform the draft [p.1]. Several civil-society actors also participated. One example is ???????AI???????? (Creators AI Association for Thinking about the Future of Art) a volunteer-based CSO supporting creators in light of evolving AI technologies (see: https://support-creators.com/start_page) which suggested to mandate for AI training to use only licensed lawful well-sourced data and to allow individuals to opt out of data use [p.97].
gmc_consult2_name: First AI Business Guidelines Review Meeting - Committee Members List
gmc_consult2_type: Open public consultation (e.g. online comments public hearing)
gmc_consult2_justif: The Guidelines received a substantial update in Ver. 1.1 reflecting in part the opinions submitted by multiple stakeholders during the review meetings. The advisory committee list includes several external experts among them the ?????????????(CCFS) (translation: Sustainability Consumer Council) which is a certified NPO (see: https://web.archive.org/web/20251121195712/https://cnrc.jp/members/members-635). Additionally the Guidelines themselves explicitly state that they were developed through input given by multiple stakeholders explicitly mentioning civil society [p.4].
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: The Guidelines are explicitly meant to be updated as a 'living document' as needed including in response to international discussions and through continued multi-stakeholder engagement [p.3]. Ver. 1.1 was itself produced after review meetings where multiple stakeholders submitted opinions and the advisory committee list includes external experts such as the ?????????????(CCFS) a certified NPO see https://web.archive.org/web/20250805150126/https://cnrc.jp/members/members-635/ . The Guidelines further state that they were developed through input from multiple stakeholders explicitly including civil society [p.4].
gmc_particip_mech1_name: Ministry of Education Culture Sports Science and Technology (MEXT) Study Group on the Use of Generative AI in Primary and Secondary Education (Chair Yuta Tonegawa the Representative Director of Minna no Code)
gmc_particip_mech1_type: Oversight advisory policy monitoring and evaluation bodies where CSOs have a seat
gmc_particip_mech1_justif: The Ministry of Education Culture Sports Science and Technology  (MEXT) established a Study Group on the Use of Generative AI in Primary and Secondary Education to review monitor and update generative-AI guidelines for schools. One of the Chairs is Yuta Tonegawa the Representative Director of Minna no Code a certified NPO focused on children’s digital literacy (see https://code.or.jp/about). Through regular meetings (every month see: https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/shingi/chousa/shotou/193/giji_list/index.htm) she can express her expert opinion and give recommendations and thus participate in AI governance in the education sector.
gmc_particip_mech2_justif: The E-GOV public comment platform gives CSOs a formal traceable channel to publicly influence national AI governance. The entry I listed under “Open public consultation”the 'Public Opinion Call on the Draft AI Business Guidelines' was actually carried out through this exact mechanism ensuring that civil society input is officially recorded and considered. Further AI-related consultations such as for example the call on the Guidelines for Procurement and Utilization of Generative AI (https://public-comment.e-gov.go.jp/pcm/1040CLASSNAME=PCM1040&Mode=1&id=290503241) demonstrate that CSOs can provide concerns suggestions and other feedback not only at the drafting stage but also during subsequent updates of AI guidelines.

gmc_title: The Government-wide vision on generative AI of the Netherlands
gmc_body_name: Algorithms Coordination Directorate (DCA) and Government AI Validation Team (Rijks Al-validatieteam)
gmc_consult1_name: Pleio (generatieveai.pleio.nl)
gmc_consult1_type: Open public consultation (e.g. online comments public hearing)
gmc_consult1_justif: Since May 2023 the government has developed an inclusive AI vision by testing outcomes across multiple sectors. Central to this was Pleio a secure open-source Dutch public sector collaboration tool which facilitated CSO and public participation via iterative insight sharing. This created a feedback loop where expert and public input from sessions was validated on the platform. To ensure diverse viewpoints a sounding board representing social perspectives and an intergovernmental working group were established. While the community link (generatieveai.pleio.nl) is now inactive its role in validating policy outcomes is confirmed in Appendix 1 (Page 50).
gmc_consult2_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult2_justif: There is no clear website or clear start/end date for the sounding board group which was created to provide direct feedback on policy drafts during 2023 (attached google drive doc)(Appendix 1 p. 50). It worked to ensure the vision has a broad societal basis by connecting stakeholders at all levels and balancing opportunities with risks regarding sustainability ethics and work. The sounding board group included representatives from CSOs like Bits of Freedom FNV(The Federation of Dutch Trade Union) and other bodies like CIO(Chief Information Officers)-Platform IPO(Interprovinciaal Overleg)  NL-AIC(Nederlandse AI Coalitie) Politie The Social and Economic Council municipal association andn employers organisation.
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: The vision uses an iterative 'learning approach' requiring 'continuously conducting a broad social dialogue' (Page 29). Active collaboration with civil society organizations is explicitly cited as essential for policy on Page 31. Under 'new actions' (Page 30) the government plans to extend dialogue with 'residents workers trade unions and entrepreneurs' and separately explores appointing a 'quartermaster' to coordinate social dialogue initiatives. CSOs also participated via a sounding board group and the Pleio community (generatieveai.pleio.nl) used to test outcomes and gather public input (Page 50).

gmc_body_name: National Centre for Artificial Intelligence and Robotics (NCAIR) and the AI Governance Regulatory Body (Pillar 5 – Objective 5.1.2 pp. 45–46). However in practice the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) working through the National Centre for Artificial Intelligence and Robotics (NCAIR) is currently coordinating activities aligned with the Strategy (see ecosystem/delivery actors on p. 16 and the high-level Roadmap for Implementation on p. 59).
gmc_consult1_name: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy Workshop
gmc_consult1_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult1_justif: The initiative enabled CSO participation in developing the National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (NAIS) through a deliberate multi-stakeholder design that co-created the strategy using a human-centred approach. The NAIS reinforces this: Page 2 acknowledges stakeholders who participated in workshops; Page 16 identifies non-profits/civil society groups as core ecosystem actors; and Section 1.8.2 (Stakeholder Consultation Process and Contributors) lists CSOs that contributed to the strategy including Data Science Nigeria (DSN) RISE Networks Nigeria Computer Society (NCS) Co-Creation Hub Africa (CcHUB) and the Global Center on AI Governance.
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: The strategy is built on a foundational 'Collaboration' principle and explicitly requires a multi-stakeholder approach for implementation including commitments from civil society (Page 5 29). This makes CSO involvement a continuous requirement for the implementation of the strategic pillars.
gmc_particip_mech1_name: National Centre for Artificial Intelligence and Robotics (NCAIR) AI Collective
gmc_particip_mech1_type: Other
gmc_particip_mech1_justif: Nigeria AI Collective is a government-initiated multi-stakeholder community of practice that convenes CSOs with policymakers to support responsible AI governance. The Ministry’s official “Week in Review” notes that the Minister formally launched the Nigeria AI Collective describing it as a community of practice to advance AI in Nigeria and support the safe and ethical use of AI. It enables CSO participation through structured engagement and capacity building (e.g. the six-month “AI for Civil Society Workshop”) helping CSOs build AI literacy develop advocacy strategies and implement accountability interventions/campaigns that feed into public engagement and policy dialogue on ethical inclusive AI.

gmc_consult1_justif: In 2023 Peru became the first country in Latin America to pass a General Law on Artificial Intelligence. In Peru laws are usually implemented through regulations. The entity responsible for drafting the regulations for the AI ??Law conducted several rounds of public consultations and virtual sessions between 2023 and 2024 to gather feedback from all stakeholders including CSOs. The final consultation took place from November 25 to December 6 2024.
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: Article 9 of the Framework indicates that the mechanism through which the participation of CSOs and other stakeholders will take place is through the 'High-Level Committee for a Digital Innovative and Competitive Peru.' This Committee is a mechanism that predates Peru's AI Law and has existed since 2018 to generate synergies among multiple stakeholders to promote government and digital transformation. From now on the Committee will include the implementation of the AI ??Law as one of its activities. The Committee has reserved seats for representatives of civil society. gmc_title: National Programme to Promote the Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Slovenia by 2025 (NpAI)
gmc_body_name: Ministry of Public Administration and the NpAI Steering Committee
gmc_consult1_name: Inter-ministerial Working Group for the Preparation of the National AI Programme 2030
gmc_consult1_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult1_justif: The Ministry of Digital Transformation established the Inter-ministerial Working Group (MDS NpUI) on March 24 2025 to include representatives from civil society academia and the private sector. This multi-stakeholder body served as the primary advisory mechanism for drafting the NpUI 2030. CSOs including the umbrella organization CNVOS were formally invited in August 2025 to participate in the strategic drafting process. This involvement culminated in the NpUI 2030 Consultation on September 16 2025 where CSOs participated in thematic workshops. Their input on ethics regulation and sovereign infrastructure was formally consolidated in the group's third meeting on September 25 2025.
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: The NpUI 2030 explicitly establishes a governance structure for monitoring and review in Section 5. A key mechanism is the Permanent Forum for AI (Stalni forum za UI) defined in Section 5.2 as an advisory body to the Government. The program emphasizes that successful implementation necessitates an 'inclusive dialogue with civil society'. Additionally Section 5.3 creates the AI Observatory (UI observatorij) to monitor indicators and propose measures based on identified social needs. These formal bodies ensure that stakeholders including civil society have a role in the ongoing oversight and adaptation of the strategy
gmc_particip_mech1_name: Inter-ministerial Working Group for the Preparation of NpUI 2030 (MDS NpUI)
gmc_particip_mech1_type: Oversight advisory policy monitoring and evaluation bodies where CSOs have a seat
gmc_particip_mech1_justif: As of September 30 2025 the primary operational mechanism is the Inter-ministerial Working Group for the Preparation of NpUI 2030 (MDS NpUI). Formally appointed on March 24 2025 this multi-stakeholder body explicitly includes representatives from civil society. Throughout 2025 this group held multiple sessions (April July and September) to advise on the national AI roadmap. Additionally the NpUI 2030 framework formally establishes the Permanent Forum for AI (Stalni forum za UI) and the AI Observatory (UI observatorij) as the ongoing operational bodies for stakeholder-led monitoring and advice.

gmc_particip_mech1_justif: As a part of the Expert Committee on Development of AI civil society experts (including Tetiana Avdieieva (DSLU) and Olga Petriv (CEDEM)) participate in drafting legislative and soft law regulations as well as advise Ukrainian Ministry for Digital Transformation on the matters related to AI governance. country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
indicator: Government Mechanisms for CSO Inclusion in AI Policy and Governance
gmc_title: A pro-innovation approach to AI regulation: government response (United Kingdom)
gmc_defence_and_security_justif: Paragraph 78 states: 'Our intention is for our regulatory framework to apply to the whole of the UK subject to existing exemptions and derogations for unique operating requirements such as defence and national security.' This confirms that military and national security applications of AI fall outside the regulatory framework's scope maintaining existing exemptions for these sensitive domains.
gmc_consult1_name: AI regulation: a pro-innovation approach - Government response to consultation
gmc_consult1_type: Open public consultation (e.g. online comments public hearing)
gmc_consult1_justif: The Department for Science Innovation and Technology (DSIT) conducted a 12-week public consultation on its AI regulation white paper. Section 6 (p.42) confirms 409 written responses from organizations and individuals plus 364 individuals engaged through roundtables and workshops. The Ministerial foreword by Secretary of State Michelle Donelan notes that 'leading voices in academia and civil society' supported the approach (p.4). DSIT commits to working 'closely with civil society industry and international partners' on future policy development (p.7) including targeted consultations on the cross-economy AI risk register and monitoring framework (p.8).
gmc_particip_provis1_justif: The “central function for AI regulation” is described in Section 3.1 as a mechanism to support implementation and build “feedback loops with… civil society.” It will coordinate regulators assess risks and engage stakeholders across sectors. This constitutes a formal structure for inclusive ongoing participation.
gmc_particip_mech1_name: Government Digital Service Responsible AI Advisory Panel
gmc_particip_mech1_type: Oversight advisory policy monitoring and evaluation bodies where CSOs have a seat
gmc_particip_mech1_justif: The GDS Responsible AI Advisory Panel is an external body set up by the Government Digital Service to “bring together views from across the public sector industry academia and civil society on the responsible use of AI.” Civil-society experts sit as panel members and give strategic advice plus “constructive challenge and scrutiny” of government AI products and approaches providing an ongoing channel for CSO oversight of public-sector AI use.

gmc_title: America´s AI Action Plan
gmc_consult1_name: Federal Registration. Request for information on the development of an Artificial Intelligence Action Plan
gmc_consult1_type: Open public consultation (e.g. online comments public hearing)
gmc_consult1_justif: SUMMARY: On behalf of the Office ofScience and Technology Policy (OSTP)the NITRD NCO requests input from allinterested parties on the Development ofan Artificial Intelligence (AI) ActionPlan (‘‘Plan’’) (p. 1). The RFI enabled CSO participation by opening an inclusive public consultation: it “seek[s] input from the public… and any other interested parties” (SUMMARY p.1). It provides clear submission channels (email/mail) and rules (=15 pages; one submission) (ADDRESSES/Instructions p.1). It also commits that “Comments received will be taken into consideration” (Supplementary Information p.2) and notes responses may be posted online/FOIA-releasable (p.2).

gmc_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of Uruguay 2024-2030
gmc_consult1_name: Public Consultation on the National Artificial Intelligence Strategy
gmc_consult1_type: Open public consultation (e.g. online comments public hearing)
gmc_consult1_justif: According to the Strategy: 'Stage 4 - Public Consultation. The document was published for public consultation from September 16 to October 21 2024 through the Digital Citizen Participation Platform allowing all interested parties to contribute.We received 28 proposals from 4 national and international civil society organizations 6 private sector organizations 1 multilateral organization and 6 individuals. These proposals resulted in 78 contributions 63% of which were incorporated in full or in part into the document while others will be included in the Roadmap that will be developed.' (p.54)
gmc_consult2_name: Dialogue table “Artificial Intelligence: opportunities and challenges of a national strategy”
gmc_consult2_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult2_justif: According to the Strategy: 'Stage 2 - Proposals. Seven dialogue and working groups were held to gather proposals related to the potential objectives and actions to be integrated into the new strategy along with discussion groups with children and adolescents.The dialogue groups included the participation of more than 300 representatives from over 40 government institutions 11 civil society organizations 45 private sector organizations academia and various stakeholder groups who contributed diverse perspectives. All reports detailing each contribution are available on the Agesic website. (p. 53).

gmc_title: Draft Law on Artificial Intelligence (Vietnam)
gmc_particip_mech1_name: D? th?o Online - Noi nhân dân tham gia vào ho?t d?ng l?p pháp c?a qu?c h?i (Online Draft Legislations - Where people participate in the legislative activities of the National Assembly)
gmc_particip_mech1_type: Other
gmc_particip_mech1_justif: The platform publishes public consultations. Anyone and any organization (including CSOs) can submit their comments online and have their comments published on the platform. This platform enables: 1) right of access to information on the Draft law (and other AI policies); 2) right to speak up and share comments on the Draft Law. Even though the platform has not yet published the responses and inclusion of CSO' feedback I know that there is a response mechanism in which the National Assembly and relevant agencies must consider and reply to all comments provided (as I have received many Draft law full packages). The platform has been active since 21st January 2007 and will continue to be active for following AI related laws.

gmc_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024 - 2026 (Zambia)
gmc_consult1_name: Stakeholder Consultation
gmc_consult1_type: Participation in multi-stakeholder working groups or advisory committees
gmc_consult1_justif: The strategy’s Stakeholder Analysis (Page 17 3.2 Assessment and Goal Setting) identifies civil society as a key stakeholder and describes a co-creation process with government academia private sector and civil society to design an inclusive AI strategy. It mentions “broad consultations with representatives from civil society” and “iterative input” from stakeholders. However specific CSOs consulted are not listed and no formal submissions or feedback summaries are provided. This Evidence supports partial involvement.
